title: Today's Markets Brief: Citi warns global stock markets are the frothiest since 2008
summary: A look at Citi's warning on global stock market valuations and the US Supreme Court's backing of federal regulators.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your markets brief. I am looking at some interesting developments in the world of finance, and I'm excited to walk you through them.

First up, Citi has issued a warning that global stock markets are looking a bit frothy - and I mean, really frothy. According to their analysis, valuations are at levels not seen since 2008. Now, that was a pretty eventful year in the markets, if you recall.

Citi's concerns are rooted in the fact that stock prices have been rising steadily, while earnings expectations have not kept pace. This has led to a bit of a disconnect between market valuations and actual earnings. My read is that investors are feeling pretty optimistic, but perhaps a tad too optimistic.

In other news, the US Supreme Court has handed down a couple of decisions that are worth noting. They've backed federal regulators in wins for the FCC and SEC. What stands out to me is that these rulings could have implications for how these agencies regulate markets and protect investors.

The court's decisions are being seen as a boost for regulatory power, which could lead to increased oversight and potentially more stringent rules for companies. I think it's interesting to see how these developments might shape the regulatory landscape.

My quick read: today's market story has that familiar split-screen feeling. There is real excitement around artificial intelligence and chips, but the room still keeps one eye on risk, because markets enjoy a celebration more when they know where the exits are.
